# Volt Nederland Track Record — Seats, Votes, Controversies 2021–2026

## Summary

Volt entered the Tweede Kamer in 2021 with three seats (2.42%), declined to two in 2023 (1.71%), and declined further to one seat in 2025 (1.10%). In every Tweede Kamer term it has been an opposition party. Its sole remaining MP is founder and party leader Laurens Dassen. The party simultaneously holds two seats in the Eerste Kamer and two MEPs elected in 2024 (5.13%), making the European Parliament its strongest institutional foothold. In the March 2026 municipal elections Volt retained two seats in Amsterdam and won its first Den Haag city council seat.

The main internal controversy of the parliamentary period was the Nilüfer Gündoğan affair (2022–2023): Volt's second elected MP was suspended and eventually expelled following 13 misconduct allegations (physical violence, unwanted sexual advances, intimidation, abuse of position). Initial court proceedings found procedural flaws in the expulsion; an appeals court in February 2023 upheld the expulsion as justified. Gündoğan sat as an independent until the November 2023 election, briefly leaving Volt with a de facto one-seat faction. The affair generated significant media coverage and exposed weaknesses in Volt's internal disciplinary processes, though the party's handling was ultimately validated by the courts.

Parliamentary activity has been consistent with platform priorities despite small numbers. Documented activities include: an initiative for the 2% NATO norm; corporate social responsibility legislation; free school meals initiative; motions on international rail policy; defense funding debates; a lobby register motion (2025); motions on the Digital Omnibus regulation; EU-level motions on Slovakia, Israel-Palestine (position evolved, see below), and UNRWA. Volt voted for UNRWA funding when a majority voted to reduce it. On Israel-Palestine, Volt's position shifted during 2025 — voting against a permanent arms embargo in June 2025 but for one by August 2025, a documented evolution the party acknowledges.

The party is growing in membership (from ~70 members at founding in 2018 to ~19,000 by early 2026) despite declining vote share in national elections. The disconnect between membership growth and electoral decline is not explained in available sources.

## Evidence

- [Volt Netherlands — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volt_Netherlands) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Overview of electoral results, leadership, Gündoğan controversy, current membership and institutional footprint.
- [Volt Nederland — nl.wikipedia.org](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Volt_Nederland) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Dutch-language version with detailed electoral timeline: 2019 EP (no seat), 2021 TK (3 seats), 2023 TK (2 seats), 2024 EP (2 seats, 5.13%), 2025 TK (1 seat), 2023 Eerste Kamer (2 seats).
- [Tweede Kamerfractie Volt — Parlement.com](https://www.parlement.com/tweede-kamerfractie-volt) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Confirms current fraction: 1 member (Dassen), opposition status, 2023 Boutkan temporary replacement during Koekkoek's maternity leave.
- [Volt Nederland — Parlement.com](https://www.parlement.com/partij/volt-nederland) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Electoral results timeline and institutional structure confirmed.
- [Volt allowed to expel MP Gündoğan — NL Times, 2023-02-07](https://nltimes.nl/2023/02/07/volt-allowed-expel-mp-gundogan-harassment-accusations-appeals-court-rules) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Appeals court ruling upholding Volt's expulsion.
- [Volt expels MP Gündoğan after 13 harassment accusations — NL Times, 2022-02-26](https://nltimes.nl/2022/02/26/volt-expels-mp-gundogan-13-harassment-accusations) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Initial expulsion, nature of allegations.
- [Nilüfer Gündoğan — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nil%C3%BCfer_G%C3%BCndo%C4%9Fan) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Full timeline of the Gündoğan case; confirms she sat as independent until 2023 without contesting the election.
- [Volt schorst Kamerlid Gündoğan — Volt Nederland](https://voltnederland.org/nieuws/volt-schorst-kamerlid-gundogan-vragen-en-antwoorden) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Volt's own Q&A on the suspension decision.
- [Terugblik 2023 — Volt Nederland](https://voltnederland.org/nieuws/terugblik-2023) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Party's own review of 2023 parliamentary work: NATO 2% initiative, corporate responsibility legislation, free school meals, maternity-leave replacement, post-election membership surge of 1,100+.
- [Laurens Dassen motions — Tweede Kamer](https://www.tweedekamer.nl/kamerleden_en_commissies/alle_kamerleden/dassen-lajm-volt/meer?activityType=moties) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Official parliamentary motions listing; December 2025 motions on Digital Omnibus conditions, obligation-to-invest abolition; Slovakia/EU proceedings motion; lobby register threat (March 2025).
- [Majority demands lobby register — NL Times, 2025-03-15](https://nltimes.nl/2025/03/15/majority-tweede-kamer-demands-lobby-register-minister-resists) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Dassen's lobby register motion achieved a parliamentary majority, demonstrating Volt's ability to build cross-party coalitions even from a single seat.
- [Palestina en Israël: wat vindt Volt? — The Rights Forum](https://rightsforum.org/palestina-en-israel-wat-vindt-volt/) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Volt's evolving Israel-Palestine position: recognised genocide from April 2025; voted against permanent arms embargo June 2025; voted for embargo August 2025. Supports South Africa's ICJ case, immediate Palestinian recognition, economic sanctions on settlements.
- [2025 Dutch general election — Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2025_Dutch_general_election) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Election context: snap election after Schoof cabinet collapse (June 2025, PVV left over asylum policy; NSC left August over Israel sanctions). Volt received 1.10%, 1 seat.
- [Current polling — PolitPro](https://politpro.eu/en/netherlands) (accessed 2026-04-19) — April 2026 polling: Volt at ~1.4% aggregate, projecting 2 seats; trend slightly upward (+0.3pp from October 2025 result).
- [March 2026 municipal elections — DutchNews.nl](https://www.dutchnews.nl/2026/03/the-results-in-the-big-five-cities-show-stability-on-the-left/) (accessed 2026-04-19) — Volt retained 2 Amsterdam seats; won first Den Haag city council seat. National 2.9% in participating municipalities.

## Uncertainty / conflicting sources

- **Reasons for declining Tweede Kamer vote share**: No systematic analysis of why Volt fell from 3 seats (2021) to 2 (2023) to 1 (2025) was found in available sources. The LSE EuroPP blog analysis of the 2025 election did not mention Volt at all, suggesting small-party volatility was below the analytical threshold. One plausible interpretation — voter consolidation toward D66 on the pro-EU left — is supported by the party composition research (see `volt-distinctive-positioning.md`) but is not confirmed by a dedicated study.
- **Gündoğan affair — severity of procedural failure**: The initial court finding that Volt's procedures were defective (requiring a court-ordered reversal and €5,000 damages) is a documented governance failure, though the substance of the expulsion was ultimately upheld. How this affected internal party culture is unknown.
- **Israel-Palestine vote inconsistency**: The June–August 2025 arms embargo position reversal is documented but the internal party decision process for this shift is not available in public sources.
- **Eerste Kamer activity**: Only the Tweede Kamer fraction (Dassen) is substantially documented in available sources. Eerste Kamer fraction chair Gaby Perin-Gopie's activity is not covered.
- **Koekkoek departure**: Marieke Koekkoek did not return as MP after the 2025 election (not on the list or not elected). Whether this reflects a deliberate party decision or other factors is unclear from available sources.
